question: What does Mary's final quoted remark reveal about her?
correctText: She felt the unfair treatment had affected her ability to trust others

rewrites.currentText: She was joking about her difficult life
rewrites.newText: She was making a lighthearted joke about her hardships and did not truly mean it

rewrites.currentText: She blamed herself for her lack of recognition
rewrites.newText: She believed her own lack of education was the reason she never received proper recognition

rewrites.currentText: She was warning other women not to pursue science
rewrites.newText: She was warning other women that a career in science would only bring them disappointment and mistrust

rewrites.currentText: She was bitter and had given up on people entirely
rewrites.newText: She had become so bitter that she had completely given up on other people and abandoned her work

question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as one of Mary's discoveries?
correctText: A Tyrannosaurus Rex tooth

rewrites.currentText: An Ichthyosaurus skeleton
rewrites.newText: The complete skeleton of an Ichthyosaurus

rewrites.currentText: A complete Plesiosaurus
rewrites.newText: The first complete Plesiosaurus, a long-necked reptile

rewrites.currentText: A Pterodactyl specimen
rewrites.newText: The first British example of a flying Pterodactyl

question: What is the author's main purpose in writing this passage?
correctText: To celebrate Mary's achievements and highlight the unfairness she faced

rewrites.currentText: To persuade readers to visit Lyme Regis
rewrites.newText: To persuade readers to visit the Jurassic Coast where her statue stands

rewrites.currentText: To explain how fossils are formed in limestone
rewrites.newText: To explain how fossils form in the Blue Lias limestone of the Dorset cliffs

rewrites.currentText: To describe the different types of prehistoric creatures
rewrites.newText: To describe the different prehistoric creatures whose fossils were found along the Dorset coast

rewrites.currentText: To argue that women are better scientists than men
rewrites.newText: To argue that women make better scientists than men and should replace them in the field

question: What made the breakthrough on 1st December 1990 so remarkable?
correctText: The two tunnels met with only centimetres of error after boring over 40 kilometres in total

rewrites.currentText: It happened ahead of schedule
rewrites.newText: The two teams finished the breakthrough far ahead of the planned schedule despite all the earlier delays

rewrites.currentText: The breakthrough happened at exactly midnight
rewrites.newText: The final section of rock was broken through at exactly midnight while millions of people watched on television

rewrites.currentText: The workers had to dig through unexpectedly hard rock
rewrites.newText: The workers had to bore their way through unexpectedly hard rock that the boring machines struggled to cut through

question: What does the phrase 'white elephant' mean in paragraph 9?
correctText: An expensive project that turns out to be useless or wasteful

rewrites.currentText: A rare and valuable treasure
rewrites.newText: A rare and precious treasure that everyone wants to own and admire

rewrites.currentText: A symbol of friendship between nations
rewrites.newText: A powerful symbol of the lasting friendship that formed between Britain and France

rewrites.currentText: A very large building or structure
rewrites.newText: An enormous and impressive structure so large that it dominates the whole landscape around it

rewrites.currentText: A construction project that takes too long
rewrites.newText: A construction project that takes far longer to finish than anyone had originally planned or expected

question: Why does the author describe the TBMs as 'mechanical moles'?
correctText: Because they burrowed through the ground like the animal does

rewrites.currentText: Because they were small and furry
rewrites.newText: Because they were small and furry like the animals they were named after

rewrites.currentText: Because they worked best in darkness
rewrites.newText: Because moles are blind and the machines could operate in complete underground darkness

rewrites.currentText: Because they were designed by a scientist called Mole
rewrites.newText: Because they were designed by a famous engineer whose surname happened to be Mole

question: Why does the author begin the passage by describing the Channel as defeating armies and wrecking ships?
correctText: To establish how formidable the barrier was, making the tunnel achievement more impressive

rewrites.currentText: To explain why the Channel is dangerous for boats
rewrites.newText: To explain why the Channel has always been such a dangerous stretch of water for boats

rewrites.currentText: To give a history of naval warfare in the Channel
rewrites.newText: To provide a detailed history of the many naval battles and wars fought across the Channel

rewrites.currentText: To argue that a bridge would have been a better solution
rewrites.newText: To argue that a bridge across the Channel would have been a far better solution than the tunnel

rewrites.currentText: To show that the tunnel was unnecessary because boats already crossed it
rewrites.newText: To show that the tunnel was completely unnecessary because ships had already been crossing the Channel for centuries

question: Based on the information in the passage, which statement is most likely to be true in the future?
correctText: The tunnel will continue to be an important transport and trade link

rewrites.currentText: The Channel Tunnel will be replaced by a bridge
rewrites.newText: The Channel Tunnel will soon be replaced by a bridge linking the two countries

rewrites.currentText: Eurotunnel will build a fourth tunnel beneath the Channel
rewrites.newText: Eurotunnel will build a fourth tunnel beneath the Channel to cope with increasing passenger demand

rewrites.currentText: Passenger numbers will decline as people prefer to fly
rewrites.newText: Passenger numbers will steadily decline in the coming years as more travellers choose to fly instead

question: Why does the author compare our knowledge of the deep sea to our knowledge of Mars?
correctText: To show how surprisingly little we know about our own oceans

rewrites.currentText: To suggest we should stop exploring space
rewrites.newText: To suggest that we should stop exploring space until we understand our oceans

rewrites.currentText: To explain why deep-sea creatures look alien
rewrites.newText: To explain why so many deep-sea creatures look strange and alien to human eyes

rewrites.currentText: To prove that space exploration is more important
rewrites.newText: To prove that exploring distant planets like Mars matters more than exploring our own deep oceans

rewrites.currentText: To argue that Mars might have oceans too
rewrites.newText: To argue that Mars might once have had deep oceans similar to the ones on Earth

question: Why is food 'desperately scarce' in the deep ocean?
correctText: Without sunlight, no plants can grow to form the base of a food chain

rewrites.currentText: The cold temperatures prevent animals from eating
rewrites.newText: The freezing temperatures near the ocean floor stop most animals from being able to eat properly

rewrites.currentText: Pollution has killed most of the organisms
rewrites.newText: Human pollution reaching the deep ocean has killed off most of the organisms that once lived there

rewrites.currentText: There are too many predators competing for food
rewrites.newText: There are far too many hungry predators all competing with one another for the same limited supply of food

question: What is the connection between deep-sea vents on Earth and Jupiter's moon Europa?
correctText: Both might have conditions that could support life

rewrites.currentText: Both are equally well-explored by scientists
rewrites.newText: Both have already been thoroughly explored and mapped by scientists

rewrites.currentText: Both are heated by the sun
rewrites.newText: Both are warmed by heat coming directly from the sun above

rewrites.currentText: Both have the same species of bacteria
rewrites.newText: Both are home to exactly the same species of heat-loving bacteria

rewrites.currentText: Both were discovered in the same year
rewrites.newText: Both were first discovered by scientists during the very same year of exploration

question: Why does the author describe the anglerfish's hunting method in detail?
correctText: To give a vivid example of how bioluminescence is used for survival

rewrites.currentText: To frighten the reader about deep-sea creatures
rewrites.newText: To frighten the reader by making deep-sea creatures seem like terrifying monsters of the deep

rewrites.currentText: To compare the anglerfish to creatures on land
rewrites.newText: To compare the anglerfish and its glowing lure with similar hunting creatures found on land

rewrites.currentText: To explain why the anglerfish is the most dangerous deep-sea predator
rewrites.newText: To explain why the anglerfish is the most dangerous and deadly predator in the deep sea

question: What is the main message of the final paragraph?
correctText: That we should be inspired by the resilience of life and motivated to protect the deep ocean

rewrites.currentText: That marine biologists are the most important scientists
rewrites.newText: That marine biologists like Sylvia Earle are the most important and valuable scientists working anywhere in the world today

rewrites.currentText: That deep-sea creatures are more dangerous than we thought
rewrites.newText: That the creatures living in the deep ocean are far more dangerous and threatening to humans than scientists had ever thought

question: What effect does the opening paragraph have on the reader?
correctText: It makes the reader feel curious by describing an alien-like world on our own planet

rewrites.currentText: It creates a relaxing, peaceful atmosphere
rewrites.newText: It creates a calm and peaceful atmosphere that makes the deep ocean feel gentle and welcoming

rewrites.currentText: It makes the reader feel guilty about pollution
rewrites.newText: It makes the reader feel guilty about all the pollution that humans have dumped into the deep ocean

rewrites.currentText: It bores the reader with too many statistics
rewrites.newText: It bores the reader by listing far too many dry statistics and numbers about the depth of the ocean

rewrites.currentText: It confuses the reader with too many scientific terms
rewrites.newText: It confuses the reader by using far too many difficult scientific terms before the topic has even been introduced

question: This passage is best described as:
correctText: A factual account of a historical event and its rediscovery

rewrites.currentText: A scientific explanation of how volcanoes work
rewrites.newText: A scientific explanation of how volcanoes form and why they erupt

rewrites.currentText: A diary entry written by a Pompeii resident
rewrites.newText: A personal diary entry written by a resident who lived through the eruption

rewrites.currentText: A persuasive text arguing for the preservation of Pompeii
rewrites.newText: A persuasive text urging readers to help fund the preservation of the ruins of Pompeii

question: What does the graffiti found on Pompeii's walls tell us?
correctText: That ordinary Romans had the same kinds of everyday thoughts and feelings as people today

rewrites.currentText: That Roman children were poorly behaved
rewrites.newText: That Roman children were badly behaved and often scribbled rude words all over the walls of the city

rewrites.currentText: That Romans had no system of writing
rewrites.newText: That the Romans had no proper system of writing and could only scratch simple pictures onto their walls

question: Why do archaeologists deliberately leave parts of Pompeii unexcavated?
correctText: They want future generations with better technology to excavate them

rewrites.currentText: They have run out of funding
rewrites.newText: They have completely run out of the funding needed to continue digging

rewrites.currentText: The remaining sections are not interesting
rewrites.newText: The sections that remain buried are not thought to contain anything interesting or valuable

rewrites.currentText: The Italian government has forbidden further digging
rewrites.newText: The Italian government has passed a law that forbids any further digging at the site

rewrites.currentText: They are afraid of disturbing the volcano
rewrites.newText: They are afraid that digging further might somehow disturb the volcano and trigger another eruption

question: What emotions would a visitor to Pompeii most likely feel when seeing the plaster body casts?
correctText: Sadness and empathy for the people who died

rewrites.currentText: Amusement at how different Romans looked
rewrites.newText: Amusement at how strange and different the ancient Romans looked

rewrites.currentText: Confusion about what the casts represent
rewrites.newText: Confusion about what the mysterious plaster casts are actually meant to represent

rewrites.currentText: Boredom because they are just plaster shapes
rewrites.newText: Boredom because the casts are just dull grey shapes made of plaster

question: Which of these statements about the eruption is NOT supported by the passage?
correctText: Most deaths were caused by buildings collapsing under the weight of ash

rewrites.currentText: The eruption continued through the night
rewrites.newText: The eruption carried on all through the night and into the following early morning

rewrites.currentText: The eruption column rose over thirty kilometres high
rewrites.newText: The towering column of ash and gas rose more than thirty kilometres up into the sky

question: Why does the author begin the passage by describing ordinary daily activities in Pompeii?
correctText: To create a contrast between normal life and the disaster that followed

rewrites.currentText: To show that Roman daily life was boring
rewrites.newText: To show that daily life in a Roman city like Pompeii was dull and boring

rewrites.currentText: To prove that the eruption happened without warning
rewrites.newText: To prove that the people of Pompeii had been warned about the volcano but ignored it

rewrites.currentText: To explain what a typical Roman city looked like
rewrites.newText: To explain in careful detail what a typical prosperous Roman city like Pompeii looked like

rewrites.currentText: To demonstrate that Romans were similar to modern people
rewrites.newText: To demonstrate that ordinary Romans lived their daily lives in much the same way as modern people do

question: Why does the author use the word 'paradoxically' in the final paragraph?
correctText: Because something that caused destruction also caused preservation — two opposite outcomes

rewrites.currentText: Because the eruption was unexpected
rewrites.newText: Because the sudden eruption was completely unexpected by everyone living in the city

rewrites.currentText: Because the author disagrees with Robert Harris
rewrites.newText: Because the author strongly disagrees with what the novelist Robert Harris wrote about the city

rewrites.currentText: Because tourists both help and harm the site
rewrites.newText: Because the many tourists who visit the site both help to fund it and slowly damage it

question: Why did Aisha arrange her books in alphabetical order?
correctText: It was a way of creating something familiar and controlled in an unfamiliar place

rewrites.currentText: Her mum told her to
rewrites.newText: Her mother, who kept insisting the move would be brilliant, had ordered her to sort them

rewrites.currentText: She needed to find a specific book quickly
rewrites.newText: She wanted to be able to find any particular book quickly once homework started at her new school

question: Why does the author describe Aisha's mum's cheerfulness as 'relentless'?
correctText: Because her mum would not stop being cheerful, even though it felt forced

rewrites.currentText: Because relentless means gentle and kind
rewrites.newText: Because the word relentless is a gentle, soothing term describing a naturally warm and patient mother

rewrites.currentText: Because her mum was trying to annoy Aisha
rewrites.newText: Because her mother repeated the phrase deliberately to irritate Aisha and wear down her objections to moving

rewrites.currentText: Because her mum was always a cheerful person
rewrites.newText: Because her mother had always been a naturally cheerful person, long before the family ever decided to move

rewrites.currentText: Because her mum was genuinely very happy about the move
rewrites.newText: Because her mother was genuinely delighted about the move to the new town and could not hide her joy

question: What kind of person is Meg?
correctText: Confident, creative, and friendly without being calculating

rewrites.currentText: Quiet, thoughtful, and shy
rewrites.newText: Quiet, reserved, and too shy to approach new people

rewrites.currentText: Popular, sporty, and competitive
rewrites.newText: Popular and sporty, endlessly competing to win at everything

rewrites.currentText: Nervous, careful, and easily upset
rewrites.newText: Nervous and careful, easily upset whenever anything goes slightly wrong

question: What does the description of Meg's friends tell us about the group?
correctText: They are individuals with different interests who accept each other

rewrites.currentText: They all love art and painting
rewrites.newText: They all share a deep love of art and painting murals together

rewrites.currentText: They are all very similar to each other
rewrites.newText: They are all very similar, sharing the same hobbies and the same personalities

question: How does Aisha's attitude towards the school uniform change throughout the passage, and what does this represent?
correctText: It changes from feeling like a costume to feeling like it belongs to her — representing her growing sense of belonging

rewrites.currentText: She stops noticing it — showing she becomes distracted by friends
rewrites.newText: She gradually stops noticing the uniform altogether, which shows that her new friends have distracted her so completely that school itself no longer matters

rewrites.currentText: She customises it to express her individuality — showing her confidence grows
rewrites.newText: She begins customising and decorating the blazer to express her individuality, which shows that her growing confidence has made her want to stand out

rewrites.currentText: She loves it from the start — showing she is excited about the new school
rewrites.newText: She adores the maroon blazer from the very first morning, which shows that she was genuinely excited about starting at her new school all along

rewrites.currentText: She hates it at first and continues to hate it — showing she never settles in
rewrites.newText: She hates the stiff new uniform at the beginning and goes on hating it right to the end, which shows that she never truly settles in

question: What is the main theme of this passage?
correctText: How new friendships can help someone find belonging in an unfamiliar place

rewrites.currentText: The difficulties of being a parent
rewrites.newText: The struggles that parents face when they move their children to a completely new town

rewrites.currentText: The importance of being good at art
rewrites.newText: How becoming skilled at painting and art can help a nervous child succeed at school

rewrites.currentText: Why moving house is always a mistake
rewrites.newText: The idea that moving house is always a serious mistake that families should try hard to avoid

question: What is the main difference between Sam and Jess's personalities?
correctText: Sam is cautious and thinks about risks; Jess is confident and acts without hesitation

rewrites.currentText: Sam is practical; Jess is lazy
rewrites.newText: Sam is practical and hard-working, while Jess is lazy and unwilling to make any real effort

rewrites.currentText: Sam loves the outdoors; Jess prefers staying at home
rewrites.newText: Sam loves being outdoors on adventures, while Jess would always rather stay safely at home indoors

question: Why had Grandpa Arthur never told anyone about the cave?
correctText: He was planning to return with proper equipment but never did

rewrites.currentText: He had forgotten about it
rewrites.newText: He had completely forgotten the cave existed in the many years since discovering it

rewrites.currentText: The passage explains this in detail
rewrites.newText: The passage sets out his exact reasons clearly and in careful, complete detail

rewrites.currentText: He was afraid of the cave
rewrites.newText: He was frightened of the dark, unexplored passage and never dared to venture inside

rewrites.currentText: He wanted to sell it to the government
rewrites.newText: He was secretly hoping to sell the valuable cave to the government for a large sum

question: What does the description of the cave floor as 'smooth, worn by water that must have flowed here thousands of years ago' tell us?
correctText: The cave is very ancient and water shaped it over a long period before finding a new path

rewrites.currentText: The cave was recently formed
rewrites.newText: The cave was formed only very recently, in the last few years, by fast-moving water that is still carving the rock

rewrites.currentText: Water still flows through the cave regularly
rewrites.newText: Water still flows regularly through the cave today, which is exactly what made the rock beneath their feet feel so smooth

rewrites.currentText: The floor is dangerous because it is slippery
rewrites.newText: The floor is dangerous because the constant water has left it wet and slippery, making it very easy for the children to fall

question: Why does Jess whisper 'It's like a palace' rather than speaking normally?
correctText: The cave was so beautiful it inspired a sense of awe and reverence

rewrites.currentText: She was afraid someone might hear them
rewrites.newText: She was afraid that someone hiding deeper inside the cave might overhear them and discover them

rewrites.currentText: Sam had asked her to be quiet
rewrites.newText: Sam had firmly told her to keep her voice down so they would not attract attention

question: How did Jess convince Sam to explore the cave?
correctText: By appealing to their grandfather's memory and what he would have wanted

rewrites.currentText: By bribing him with sweets
rewrites.newText: By bribing him with sweets and promising to share her pocket money afterwards

rewrites.currentText: By threatening to go without him
rewrites.newText: By threatening to go into the dark cave alone if he refused to come with her

rewrites.currentText: By promising it would be completely safe
rewrites.newText: By promising him that exploring the cave would be completely safe and free of any real danger

rewrites.currentText: By saying their parents had given permission
rewrites.newText: By telling him that their parents had already given full permission for the two of them to explore

question: What does Sam's decision at the end of the passage reveal about his character development?
correctText: He is still cautious but the experience has made him willing to push beyond his comfort zone

rewrites.currentText: He has completely overcome his fears
rewrites.newText: He has completely overcome every one of his fears and now feels no anxiety whatsoever about any kind of danger

rewrites.currentText: He feels obligated to finish what his grandfather started
rewrites.newText: He feels a heavy sense of duty to finish the exploration that his grandfather began but was never able to complete

rewrites.currentText: He has become reckless and no longer cares about danger
rewrites.newText: He has become completely reckless and no longer cares at all about the danger, throwing away the caution that once defined him

question: What effect does the author create by describing the cave formations in such vivid detail in paragraphs 9 and 10?
correctText: It creates a sense of wonder and helps the reader visualise the extraordinary beauty of the cave

rewrites.currentText: It makes the cave sound dangerous and unstable
rewrites.newText: It makes the cave sound dangerous and unstable, as though the heavy stone formations might suddenly collapse onto the children

rewrites.currentText: It proves that the author has visited many caves
rewrites.newText: It proves that the author must have personally visited a great many real caves in order to describe them so accurately

question: Why does Noor rule out Morse code?
correctText: The pattern does not match any standard Morse character

rewrites.currentText: She does not know Morse code
rewrites.newText: She has never learned Morse code and cannot read it at all

rewrites.currentText: Morse code uses sound, not light
rewrites.newText: Morse code can only be sent using sound, never using flashes of light

rewrites.currentText: She thinks Morse code is too old-fashioned
rewrites.newText: She thinks Morse code is far too old-fashioned for anyone to be using now

rewrites.currentText: The signals were too slow for Morse code
rewrites.newText: The flashing signals appeared far too slowly to possibly be genuine Morse code

question: How does Noor feel about living in Pencarrow?
correctText: She has not fully settled but has found ways to cope with the isolation

rewrites.currentText: She does not care where she lives
rewrites.newText: She does not care in the slightest where she lives, feeling equally at home anywhere

rewrites.currentText: She loves it and has many close friends
rewrites.newText: She absolutely loves the village and has already made a large circle of close, loyal friends there

rewrites.currentText: She hates it and wants to move back immediately
rewrites.newText: She hates living in Pencarrow so much that she is desperate to move straight back to her old home

question: What is Noor's breakthrough at the end of the passage?
correctText: She realises the inscription letters are musical notes, not code letters, and the light signals represent a melody

rewrites.currentText: She deciphers a Morse code message
rewrites.newText: She finally manages to decipher a hidden Morse code message that spells out the name of the mysterious signaller

rewrites.currentText: She discovers who is sending the signals
rewrites.newText: She works out exactly who has been climbing the lighthouse each night to send the mysterious flashing signals across the bay

question: Why does the author reveal details about the light pattern gradually across several paragraphs rather than all at once?
correctText: To build suspense and mirror the step-by-step process of solving a mystery

rewrites.currentText: To make the passage longer
rewrites.newText: To make the passage much longer so that it fills more space on the page

rewrites.currentText: Because the author forgot to include them earlier
rewrites.newText: Because the author simply forgot to include all of the details together earlier in the story

rewrites.currentText: To test whether the reader is paying attention
rewrites.newText: To test whether the reader is paying close enough attention to remember all the earlier clues

rewrites.currentText: Because Noor was confused and needed time to think
rewrites.newText: Because Noor herself was confused by the pattern and needed a long time to think it all through

question: Why does the passage end with 'Noor was going to find out who' rather than revealing the answer?
correctText: To create a cliffhanger that makes the reader want to read on

rewrites.currentText: To frustrate the reader deliberately
rewrites.newText: To deliberately frustrate and annoy the reader by refusing to give them any answer

rewrites.currentText: Because the author does not know who it is
rewrites.newText: Because the author has not yet decided who is sending the signals from the lighthouse

rewrites.currentText: Because who is sending the signal is not important
rewrites.newText: Because the identity of the person sending the signals does not really matter to the story

question: Where does Boudicca's statue stand today?
correctText: Victoria Embankment, near Westminster Bridge

rewrites.currentText: Trafalgar Square
rewrites.newText: In Trafalgar Square, close to Nelson's Column

rewrites.currentText: Buckingham Palace
rewrites.newText: In the gardens outside Buckingham Palace, the royal residence

question: Why did King Prasutagus name the Roman emperor as co-heir in his will?
correctText: Because he hoped it would protect his family from Roman aggression after his death

rewrites.currentText: Because Roman law required it
rewrites.newText: Because Roman law compelled every conquered king to leave part of his kingdom to the emperor

question: Why did Suetonius Paulinus choose a narrow valley for the final battle?
correctText: The confined space would neutralise Boudicca's huge numerical advantage

rewrites.currentText: It was close to his supply base
rewrites.newText: It sat close to the supply base where his legions were provisioned

rewrites.currentText: It was near London and easy to reach
rewrites.newText: It lay near London, making it quick for his forces to reach

rewrites.currentText: The valley had water supplies for his soldiers
rewrites.newText: The valley contained fresh streams that would keep his ten thousand soldiers supplied

rewrites.currentText: He wanted to trap Boudicca's army from behind
rewrites.newText: He intended to surround Boudicca's warriors from behind and cut off their retreat

question: What does the 'destruction layer' found in Colchester prove?
correctText: That a catastrophic fire occurred at this location during Boudicca's rebellion

rewrites.currentText: That the archaeology is unreliable
rewrites.newText: That the archaeological evidence from Colchester is confused and cannot be trusted

rewrites.currentText: That Boudicca's army used advanced weapons
rewrites.newText: That Boudicca's warriors must have been armed with unusually advanced weapons for the period

rewrites.currentText: That Colchester was built by the Romans
rewrites.newText: That Colchester was originally built and settled by the Romans as a colony for soldiers

question: What does Suetonius Paulinus's decision to abandon London suggest about him?
correctText: He was a pragmatic military commander who prioritised winning the war over saving one city

rewrites.currentText: He was tricked by Boudicca into leaving
rewrites.newText: He had been outwitted and deceived by one of Boudicca's clever schemes into abandoning the city

rewrites.currentText: He was a coward who ran from danger
rewrites.newText: He was a frightened coward who fled from the approaching danger to save only his own life

question: What motivated Boudicca's rebellion?
correctText: Fury at the cruel treatment of herself and her daughters, and the theft of her kingdom

rewrites.currentText: Religious differences with the Romans
rewrites.newText: A determination to defend the ancient Celtic religion and its priests against the new beliefs the Romans were imposing

question: Why does the author describe the placement of Boudicca's statue beside Parliament as 'deeply symbolic'?
correctText: Because it is ironic that a woman who destroyed London now watches over its seat of government

rewrites.currentText: Because the statue is the largest in London
rewrites.newText: Because the enormous bronze statue is the largest and most impressive monument anywhere in the whole of London

rewrites.currentText: Because Parliament is the most visited building in London
rewrites.newText: Because the Houses of Parliament are the most visited and most photographed tourist building in the entire city of London

rewrites.currentText: Because Boudicca was the first woman to sit in Parliament
rewrites.newText: Because Boudicca is remembered and honoured there as the very first woman ever to take her seat inside the English Parliament

rewrites.currentText: Because the statue was built by the same people who built Parliament
rewrites.newText: Because the same craftsmen and architects who constructed the Houses of Parliament were also responsible for designing and casting the statue

question: What is the author's main purpose in this passage?
correctText: To present a vivid, balanced account of a pivotal event in British history

rewrites.currentText: To describe archaeological methods used at Colchester
rewrites.newText: To explain in detail the archaeological methods used to excavate the destruction layer at Colchester

rewrites.currentText: To argue that the Romans should never have invaded Britain
rewrites.newText: To argue forcefully that the Roman invasion of Britain was wrong and should never have happened

rewrites.currentText: To explain why Roman civilisation was superior to Celtic culture
rewrites.newText: To explain why Roman civilisation was far more advanced and superior to the culture of the Celtic tribes

question: What effect does the opening sentence create?
correctText: It immediately grabs attention by showing how one woman challenged the world's greatest empire

rewrites.currentText: It creates a peaceful, calm atmosphere
rewrites.newText: It creates a peaceful and calm atmosphere that gently settles the reader before the story begins

rewrites.currentText: It bores the reader with a date
rewrites.newText: It bores the reader by opening with a dull historical date instead of any real excitement

question: Where is the Lambton estate located?
correctText: Near the River Wear in County Durham

rewrites.currentText: Near the River Thames
rewrites.newText: Beside the River Thames in the south of England

question: What was John Lambton doing when he caught the creature?
correctText: Fishing instead of going to church

rewrites.currentText: Walking his dog
rewrites.newText: Taking his father's greyhound for a walk

rewrites.currentText: Exploring a cave
rewrites.newText: Exploring a dark cave in the nearby hills

rewrites.currentText: Hunting in the forest
rewrites.newText: Hunting wild animals in the forest near the estate

rewrites.currentText: Swimming in the river
rewrites.newText: Swimming in the cold waters of the River Wear one morning

question: Why did John's plan to kill the greyhound fail?
correctText: John's father forgot the plan and ran out before the dog was released

rewrites.currentText: The greyhound escaped from the castle
rewrites.newText: The greyhound broke free and escaped from the castle before the horn signal was given

rewrites.currentText: The greyhound was faster than John's father
rewrites.newText: The greyhound ran faster than John's father and reached the rock first after the horn

rewrites.currentText: John could not find his sword after the battle
rewrites.newText: John was unable to find his sword after the exhausting battle and could not kill the animal

question: How had John Lambton changed by the time he returned from the Crusades?
correctText: He had matured and was willing to take responsibility for his past mistake

rewrites.currentText: He had forgotten about the creature entirely
rewrites.newText: He had completely forgotten about the creature he once threw into the well as a boy

rewrites.currentText: He had become even more reckless and irresponsible
rewrites.newText: He had grown even more reckless and irresponsible than he had been in his wild youth

question: Why could John not bring himself to kill his father?
correctText: Natural love and morality prevented him from killing his own parent

rewrites.currentText: His father was wearing armour
rewrites.newText: His father was protected by thick armour that no ordinary weapon could pierce

question: Why does the author say the curse was 'finally fulfilled' when the ninth Lord Lambton died?
correctText: Because the wise woman had specified nine generations, and this was the ninth

rewrites.currentText: Because the Worm returned and killed him
rewrites.newText: Because the Worm returned from the river after many years and finally killed the ninth lord

rewrites.currentText: Because the family ran out of heirs
rewrites.newText: Because after the ninth lord died there were no heirs left to continue the Lambton family line

rewrites.currentText: Because the ninth lord was the first to die peacefully
rewrites.newText: Because the ninth lord was the very first Lambton to die peacefully and quietly in his own bed

rewrites.currentText: Because the ninth lord broke the curse with a magic spell
rewrites.newText: Because the ninth lord discovered a powerful magic spell that finally broke the ancient family curse forever

question: What does the author's tone in the first paragraph suggest about how they view the legend?
correctText: They are affectionately amused by it while acknowledging it is probably not factual

rewrites.currentText: They are frightened by the story
rewrites.newText: They are genuinely frightened by the gruesome story and warn readers to take it seriously

rewrites.currentText: They think it should be forgotten
rewrites.newText: They believe the old legend is embarrassing and should quietly be forgotten by the local people

rewrites.currentText: They believe every detail is historically accurate
rewrites.newText: They firmly believe that every single detail of the legend is completely true and historically accurate

question: What effect does the detail of the father running out 'arms outstretched, tears streaming down his face' create?
correctText: It creates sympathy because his reaction is completely natural — a father relieved his son is alive

rewrites.currentText: It shows that the father never loved John
rewrites.newText: It shows that the father never truly loved John and cared nothing for whether he lived or died

rewrites.currentText: It creates humour because the father's behaviour is comical
rewrites.newText: It creates humour because the father's clumsy and comical behaviour makes the reader laugh out loud at the whole scene

question: Why were red kites protected by law in medieval London?
correctText: Because they performed a useful role cleaning the streets of waste

rewrites.currentText: Because they were already very rare
rewrites.newText: Because they had already become extremely rare and needed protection from hunters

question: This passage is best described as:
correctText: An informative account of a wildlife conservation programme

rewrites.currentText: A scientific paper about bird genetics
rewrites.newText: A technical scientific paper focused entirely on red kite genetics

rewrites.currentText: A fictional story about a bird rescue
rewrites.newText: A fictional adventure story about the rescue of a single bird

rewrites.currentText: A persuasive letter arguing against game shooting
rewrites.newText: A persuasive letter written to campaign against the practice of game shooting

rewrites.currentText: A travel guide to the Chiltern Hills
rewrites.newText: A travel guide describing places to visit across the beautiful Chiltern Hills

question: Why were the Welsh kites slow to recover on their own?
correctText: The remaining population was so inbred that fertility was low and many eggs failed

rewrites.currentText: Welsh farmers refused to protect them
rewrites.newText: Welsh farmers refused to protect the birds and instead drove them away from all their land

rewrites.currentText: There were too many predators in Wales
rewrites.newText: There were far too many other predators in the remote Welsh valleys that hunted and competed with them

rewrites.currentText: There was not enough food in Wales
rewrites.newText: There was simply not enough food in the remote Welsh oak valleys to support a steadily growing population

question: Why did conservationists import kites from Sweden and Spain rather than using Welsh kites?
correctText: The Welsh population was too small and inbred to provide healthy birds for new sites

rewrites.currentText: Welsh kites were a different species
rewrites.newText: The Welsh kites belonged to a completely different species that could not breed with the others

rewrites.currentText: Foreign kites were cheaper to obtain
rewrites.newText: Importing young kites from Sweden and Spain turned out to be far cheaper than capturing the protected Welsh birds

question: What do the satellite tracking data reveal about kite behaviour?
correctText: That individual birds can travel over three hundred kilometres from their release sites

rewrites.currentText: That kites avoid urban areas completely
rewrites.newText: That kites completely avoid all urban areas and never fly near towns or busy roads

question: What does the phrase 'carrying capacity of the available habitat' mean in paragraph 11?
correctText: The maximum number of kites the environment can sustainably feed and house

rewrites.currentText: The distance kites can carry food
rewrites.newText: The maximum distance a kite is able to carry food back to its nest

rewrites.currentText: The maximum weight the land can support
rewrites.newText: The maximum total weight of birds and animals that the land is able to support

rewrites.currentText: The amount of pollution the habitat can absorb
rewrites.newText: The amount of pollution and waste that the surrounding habitat is able to safely absorb

rewrites.currentText: The number of species that can live together
rewrites.newText: The total number of different bird and animal species that can live together in one shared habitat

question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a threat to red kites?
correctText: Loss of habitat to housing development

rewrites.currentText: Illegal poisoning
rewrites.newText: Illegal poisoning by gamekeepers on shooting estates

rewrites.currentText: Vehicle collisions
rewrites.newText: Fatal collisions with vehicles on busy roads and motorways

rewrites.currentText: Competition with buzzards
rewrites.newText: Growing competition for food with the expanding buzzard population
